title: Word Search
subtitle: {key:*} / {key:#} — search for the word under the cursor without typing it.
summary: Put the cursor on a word, press * to search forward for the next occurrence, # for the previous. g* and g# do the same but without word boundaries.

blocks.text: Cursor on a word, press {key:*}, jump to the next occurrence. Press {key:#}, jump to the previous. No typing the pattern, no closing {key:Enter}.

blocks.label: Next occurrence of word under cursor
Previous occurrence of word under cursor

blocks.title: What * Actually Does
blocks.text:
{key:*} is **not** "search for the literal text under the cursor." It's: take the keyword under the cursor, wrap it in `\<…\>` (whole-word boundaries), and run that as a forward search.

So with the cursor on `foo` in `foo barfoo bazfoo`, {key:*} only matches the standalone `foo`s, not the `foo` inside `barfoo`. That's almost always what you want.

When it isn't — for example, refactoring an identifier where you also want to catch `getFoo` and `Foobar` — use {key:g}{key:*} (no boundaries). It builds the pattern without `\<…\>`.

After {key:*}, the search is in the `/` register, so you can edit it: {key:q}{key:/} opens the search history window and lets you tweak the pattern.

{key:*} is the start of one of the most useful refactor patterns: {key:*}-{key:c}{key:g}{key:n}-type-replacement-{key:Esc}-{key:.}-{key:.}-… Walk through every match, change-or-skip with {key:n} or {key:.}. See [`patterns.cgn-dot`](#patterns.cgn-dot).

blocks.title: {key:*} synthesizes a regex from the cursor token
blocks.text: Word search is a tiny code generator. It tokenizes the text under the cursor, wraps it in word-boundary anchors (`\<...\>`), and writes the result into the search-pattern slot. {key:#} is the same generator with reversed initial direction. The `g`-prefixed variants ({key:g}{key:*}, {key:g}{key:#}) drop the boundary anchors. Knowing this, you can predict exactly which token will be selected and why partial matches behave as they do.
